ema.author: European Medicines Agency (HMPC)
ema.title: European Union herbal monograph on Agrimonia eupatoria L., herba (Agrimoniae herba)

review2022.title: Agrimonia eupatoria L.: An integrative perspective on ethnomedicinal use, phenolic composition and pharmacological activity

actions.action: Anti-inflammatory gargle (mild inflammation of the mouth and throat / sore throat)
Vulnerary (minor skin inflammation and small superficial wounds, topical)

constituents.name: Flavonoids (luteolin, apigenin, quercetin and their glycosides)

contraindications.note: Traditional-use indication only: if diarrhoea persists longer than 3-4 days, or symptoms of mouth/throat inflammation persist beyond about a week, a doctor should be consulted.
Use during pregnancy and breastfeeding is not recommended, as safety has not been established.
Tannin-rich; very high or prolonged intake may cause gastrointestinal upset or constipation and can reduce the absorption of iron and some medicines taken at the same time.

internal_notes: Enriched 2026-06-12 from scientific and general literature (EMA monograph, Herbal Reality, peer-reviewed review). Originally a skeleton imported from the omniasana.bio Symptom-to-Plant Lookup tool (diarrhea, sore-throat, indigestion, wounds); symptom associations retained and now part-mapped and cited.

moro2024.title: Cranberry Juice, Cranberry Tablets, or Liquid Therapies for Urinary Tract Infection: A Systematic Review and Network Meta-analysis

llano2020.author: Gonzalez de Llano, D., Moreno-Arribas, M.V. and Bartolome, B.
llano2020.title: Cranberry Polyphenols and Prevention against Urinary Tract Infections: Relevant Considerations

nccih.author: National Center for Complementary and Integrative Health (NCCIH)
nccih.title: Cranberry: Usefulness and Safety

actions.action: Prevention of recurrent urinary tract infections (UTI), especially in women
Anti-adhesion of uropathogenic Escherichia coli to the urinary tract lining

constituents.name: A-type proanthocyanidins (PACs)
constituents.note: The constituents responsible for the anti-adhesion effect against uropathogenic E. coli.

constituents.name: Phenolic acids and organic acids (quinic, malic, citric)
constituents.note: Contribute to the acidity and antioxidant capacity.

constituents.name: Oxalates
constituents.note: Present in relatively high amounts; relevant to kidney-stone risk.

contraindications.note: Best supported for PREVENTION of recurrent UTIs, not for treating an active infection; an established UTI needs proper medical assessment and, usually, antibiotics.
May interact with the anticoagulant warfarin (evidence is conflicting); people taking warfarin should be cautious and consult their clinician.
High in oxalates; people with a history of kidney stones should avoid large or prolonged intake. Many cranberry juices are also high in sugar.

internal_notes: Enriched 2026-06-12 from scientific and general literature (systematic review/network meta-analysis, mechanistic review, NCCIH). Originally a skeleton imported from the omniasana.bio Symptom-to-Plant Lookup tool (uti); the association is retained and now cited.

zeng2021.title: Andrographolide: A review of its pharmacology, pharmacokinetics, toxicity and clinical trials and pharmaceutical researches

gonde2023.author: Gonde, D.P., Bhole, B.K. and Kakad, K.S.
gonde2023.title: Andrographolide, diterpenoid constituent of Andrographis paniculata: Review on botany, phytochemistry, molecular docking analysis, and pharmacology

actions.action: Symptomatic relief of the common cold and acute upper respiratory tract infection (reduces symptom severity and duration)

constituents.name: Diterpenoid lactones
constituents.note: Andrographolide is the principal active compound; also neoandrographolide and deoxyandrographolide.

contraindications.note: Avoid in pregnancy and when trying to conceive: high doses have shown reproductive toxicity in animals and the herb has a traditional reputation as an abortifacient.
High doses have caused nephrotoxicity and reproductive toxicity in studies; use standardized products at recommended doses and avoid prolonged high-dose use.
May lower blood pressure and blood sugar and may interact with anticoagulant/antiplatelet, antihypertensive and immunosuppressant medicines; allergic reactions can occur.

internal_notes: Enriched 2026-06-12 from scientific and general literature (PLoS ONE meta-analysis, Phytotherapy Research and Annales Pharmaceutiques Francaises reviews, MSKCC About Herbs). Originally a skeleton imported from the omniasana.bio Symptom-to-Plant Lookup tool (cold, sore-throat, fever, immune-support); associations retained and now part-mapped and cited.

kaur2021.author: Kaur, A. and Bhatti, R.
kaur2021.title: Understanding the phytochemistry and molecular insights to the pharmacology of Angelica archangelica L. (garden angelica) and its bioactive components

patel2023.author: Patel, D.K. and Patel, K.
patel2023.title: Biological Potential and Pharmacological Activity of Columbianetin in Chronic Diseases

actions.action: Carminative and aromatic bitter digestive (indigestion, bloating, flatulence)

constituents.name: Coumarins and furanocoumarins
constituents.note: Including columbianetin, angelicin, imperatorin and xanthotoxin.

constituents.name: Essential (volatile) oil
constituents.note: Rich in monoterpenes such as alpha-pinene and beta-phellandrene; gives the aromatic, carminative character.

contraindications.note: Contains furanocoumarins, which can cause photosensitivity; avoid excessive sun or UV exposure while using the herb medicinally.
Medicinal doses should be avoided during pregnancy and breastfeeding.

Coumarin constituents may interact with anticoagulant medicines, and furanocoumarins with photosensitising drugs; when wild-harvesting, take great care to distinguish garden angelica from poisonous Apiaceae look-alikes such as hemlock.

internal_notes: Enriched 2026-06-12 from scientific literature (Phytotherapy Research review of A. archangelica; review of its coumarin columbianetin). Originally a skeleton imported from the omniasana.bio Symptom-to-Plant Lookup tool (indigestion, bloating, anxiety, cold); associations retained and now part-mapped and cited.

kriplani2017.title: Arnica montana L. - a plant of healing: review

gaertner2021.author: Gaertner, K., Baumgartner, S. and Walach, H.
gaertner2021.title: Is Homeopathic Arnica Effective for Postoperative Recovery? A Meta-analysis of Placebo-Controlled and Active Comparator Trials

actions.action: Topical anti-inflammatory for bruising, swelling and sprains
Topical analgesic for muscle soreness and pain

constituents.name: Sesquiterpene lactones (helenalin, dihydrohelenalin)
constituents.note: The main anti-inflammatory principles; also responsible for the plant's toxicity and contact-allergy potential.

constituents.name: Essential oil, phenolic acids and coumarins
constituents.note: Aromatic and supporting constituents of the flower.

constituents.name: Pyrrolizidine alkaloids (trace)
constituents.note: Present in small amounts; relevant to the caution against internal use.

contraindications.note: EXTERNAL USE ONLY on unbroken skin. Arnica is toxic if swallowed and can cause stomach pain, vomiting, diarrhoea, breathing difficulty, irregular heartbeat and, in serious cases, cardiac arrest; the only safe oral form is the highly diluted homeopathic preparation.
Do not apply to broken or damaged skin, open wounds, eyes or mucous membranes.
As a member of the daisy family (Asteraceae) it can cause allergic contact dermatitis; prolonged topical use can also irritate the skin.
Avoid medicinal use during pregnancy and breastfeeding.

internal_notes: Enriched 2026-06-12 from scientific and general literature (J Pharm Pharmacol review, Frontiers in Surgery meta-analysis, Poison Control). Originally a skeleton imported from the omniasana.bio Symptom-to-Plant Lookup tool (bruising, pain, inflammation, muscle-soreness); associations retained and now part-mapped and cited. NOTE: external use only - see contraindications.
